Intermediate Scripting 4  
Now that we're bumping into the glass ceiling with our placed enemy,  
we're going to introduce one of the most valuable design tools we have:  
[set]s, and actually start exploring how we could expand our little level  
TFB Tool  
Handbook  
into a real game. We'll turn our Enemy Manager into an enemy  
spawner, set up our player to interact with multiple enemies, and learn  
how to use [set]s to build powerful scripts. In this section, you'll learn  
about:

An Enemy Spawner  
Since we set up our Enemy Manager, we actually are fairly close to having an enemy spawner. The primary  
diﬀerence between the manager and the spawner is that we'll be templating the enemy, using the clone  
command to create a copy of it, then making a new copy when the old one is killed. It's not too far oﬀ from how  
the player's projectiles work!  
Start by templating the Enemy Actor.  
Open the Enemy Manager's script.  
1 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Remove the line where we reset and add a new clone command to clone the enemy. We'll want to hang on  
to the cloned enemy, so make an actor *reference and set it, as well.  
Remove the line where we set the Enemy Actor's tint. Since we're cloning a copy of the template and  
won't be aﬀecting the original actor, we don't need it.  
In the check enemy behavior, change the control line to attempt to control our actor *reference instead.  
Now we've changed the enemy manager's logic to be more of a spawner. It creates a copy of the enemy  
whenever it cannot control it, and, though that copy may alter itself (e.g. setting its tint), the original template  
is never changed. This is much more eﬀective and ﬂexible, especially since we can now make multiple enemies  
instead of just one - something you might actually want to do in a game.  
Try building and launching and see what happens.

Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Dealing with Clones  
If you built and launched after setting up the enemy spawner, you'll probably notice that the player is unable to  
hurt the enemy. The problem is that we are cloning the Enemy Actor, but the Player Projectile's script is  
looking, explicitly, for collision with Enemy Actor, not a clone of it.  
Open the Player Projectile actor's script and note the line where we check for collision with the enemy.  
What we really want to do is see if we've hit a clone of Enemy Actor.  
Pull down on the collision check value line, then dig in to the (ACTORS) > Enemy Actor > submenu  
and note the [clones] set.  
The [clones] set is a set provided by the system for every object which contains all objects that are clones of  
the object. The system manages adding to and removing from the set. In this case, any time we clone the  
Enemy Actor, the created actor will automatically go into the Enemy Actor's set of [clones]. When the object  
is removed, it automatically is removed from the [clones] set.  
Change the check value and ﬁnd variable lines, respectively, to check for collision with and ﬁnd a  
variable ﬁrst in Enemy Actor's [clones].  
Now the projectile will try to check collision with the ﬁrst clone of Enemy Actor. If you live update, you'll note  
that the player's projectile will properly collide and damage the Enemy Actor clone. What we've just  
implemented is a very weak solution, though. It only checks for the ﬁrst clone in the set, so if there were more  
than one clone of Enemy Actor, it would ignore all of them but the ﬁrst. To prove this point, let's make another  
placement of the Enemy Manager so we get a second clone of the enemy.  
Select the Enemy Manager in the actor panel and click the Dup button to duplicate it.  
3 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Duplicating a placement is behaves similarly to making a new one in that it will be placed at the focal point and  
also will break your live updating. When you duplicate something, you inherit all the properties set in the actor  
panel, though - the new actor, in this case, will have the same script and collision/movement properties.  
NOTE: Multiple actors can all run the same script. Each one runs the same script but keeps its own copy  
of the pertinent local variables and things like behaviors. The script is the same for each actor, but the  
state of the script can vary from placement to placement.  
At this point, you should build and launch to see how things behave. There will be two enemies running  
around, but you may notice that the player's projectile can only hurt one of them. When that one dies, it will be  
able to hurt the other one. That is because the [clones] set now includes two members, and the projectile is  
set up to only look at one. When that one goes away, for example, when you kill it, it's removed from the  
[clones] set and the ﬁrst in [clones] will now refer to the other cloned enemy. What we need are some  
improved script methods for operating on sets.  
For Each  
The general issue that sets introduce is the problem that we can't just look in one place to get the information  
we want. We can't check for collision with just one Enemy Actor clone since there might be many. The real  
issue is: how do we run the same check on multiple things? The most straightforward method is the for each  
command, which lets you run some script once for every member in a set.  
Add a new for each command above the collision check in the player projectile, and set the ﬁrst  
parameter to be (ACTORS) > Enemy Actor > [clones].  
Look at the structure of the new command. It has an indentation like many of our other commands, but the  
ﬂow section of the indentation is diﬀerent. It doesn't say ﬂow continue like usual; instead, it says ﬂow for  
each and lacks a changeable parameter. This indicates that the script will have a special ﬂow here. In this  
case, it means that the interior section of the for each will run once for every member of the Enemy Actor's  
[clones] set. The for each command provides a scoped reference, ^current in [for each] that will point at  
the current member of the set as it runs through.  
Add a new print command inside the for each and have it print the ^current in [for each].  
If you live update, you should see the print appearing twice. Once for each member of the [clones] set. If you  
get the enemies to chase you around, you'll see their XYZ coordinates changing in the prints - it is, indeed,  
printing each individual one out. Now we can actually do something useful with the for each and check if  
we've hit any of the clones.  
Move the check value block into the for each block.  
4 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Change the check value and ﬁnd variable to reference ^current in [for each].  
Now we have a design question and an implementation oddity. Speaking about the oddity, if the projectile were  
to collide with both clones at the same time, it would run the remove myself line twice, which is possibly  
harmless but may interfere with the projectile's ability to check collision on the second run through in our for  
each. The design question is: Should one projectile be able to hit more than one enemy?  
If you wanted to answer no, then we could solve the oddity just by changing the ﬂow continue after the  
remove line to a ﬂow end. Then the for each would stop looping and no further script would run at all. In this  
case, however, I'd like to allow the projectile to hit multiple enemies at once, so I would like to solve the  
implementation oddity.  
Find Subset  
Now for my absolute favorite part of the program, where you'll be introduced to the absolute coolest script  
command in the whole language. Brace yourself!  
While for each lets us run a check value on each member of Enemy Actor's [clones], what if we could run a  
check value on the entire set all at once? Enter ﬁnd subset, which is entirely analogous to check value,  
except you can use it to check a whole set. You specify the set to check and the parameters for the check value  
component, then it gives you a scoped ^[subset] of members which succeed at the check.  
Above the for each line, add a new ﬁnd subset comand.  
Change the ﬁrst parameter of the lefthand side to ﬁnd a subset of (ACTORS) > Enemy Actor > [clones]  
> whose > collision.  
5 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Change the second parameter to be with myself.  
Change the righthand side to check if the collision is 1: true.  
The ﬁnd subset line, in plain English, is saying: give me all the members of Enemy Actor's [clones] who are  
colliding with me. Find subset, just like check value can fail (and can have an else or even else-if), but, if it  
succeeds, it means it found as least one member of the set that passed the check.  
Move the for each command block into the ﬁnd subset command block.  
Adjust the for each command block to run for each in ^[subset], since we'll only want this to happen for  
every member we've actually collided with.  
Now, move the ﬁnd variable line out of the collision check and right below the for each (and delete that  
old print while you're at it).  
6 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Move the remove command out of the collision check and the for each, so that it only runs after the for  
each completes.  
Finally, delete the old, unused collision check value line. The ﬁnal block of script should look as follows.  
What we've eﬀectively done is ﬂipped the order when our check and loop happens. With the old, for each only  
method, we said "loop over every enemy clone, then if I'm colliding with it, then do stuﬀ". In this case, we're  
saying "if i'm colliding with some clones, loop over each one I collide with, and do stuﬀ". Find subset lets us  
package the check value as a ﬁlter on a whole set very eﬀiciently.  
Beyond just ﬂipping some words around, we have improved upon the old logic immensely! If you think about  
optimization meaning having the fewest lines of script run that you possibly can, it's way better. In the old  
method, we'd have to run that check value on each actor individually. In the new method, we run the check  
value as part of our subset - once - and then run the ﬁnd variable on each member of the subset, as  
necessary.  
Finally, we've solved the weird behavior/design-issue involving the remove happening twice/early. Now, we can  
collide with as many enemies as we want, then we tell each one that it's been hit, and, ﬁnally, after that's all  
done, we remove the projectile.  
Fixing the Player's Autoaim  
You may have noticed some strange behavior with the player's autoaim when he shoots in certain locations. His  
script has not yet been updated to pay attention to the possibility of clones, and he'll always try to autoaim at  
that templated-out Enemy Actor placement. Not what we want, so let's make a quick tweaks to his check fov  
line.  
Open the Player Actor's script and locate the check fov line.  
7 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Change the for Enemy Actor to read for Enemy Actor's [clones].  
Now the check fov will actually look within all of the clones (you can see it actually bears some resemblance  
to ﬁnd subset). The way check fov works, it will order the [fov] set it returns by distance, starting with the  
closest going out to the farthest. In this case, the ﬁrst in ^[fov] will be the closest enemy we ﬁnd.  
Spawning Multiple Enemies  
Now, I don't know about you, but the ﬁrst thing I wanted to do when I learned about the clone command was  
make as many things as I could. "Wait, I can clone an enemy? I'm going to make one thousand!" That  
promptly crashed the game, but, still, it shows some useful lessons. Our enemy spawners are set up to only  
spawn a single enemy per placement, but wouldn't it be nice if each spawner could create more than that? We  
need some way of maintaning the number of clones each one is allowed to make, just like each one currently  
doesn't make another enemy if there's already one active.  
Creating and Using [set] Variables  
We've used the set *reference and check *reference commands to assign and check reference variables, and  
now we've begun to make use of [set]s with the for each and ﬁnd subset commands. The [clones] set is  
provided to us by the system, but we've run into a case where we need to make our own. In the case of our  
enemy spawners, just like we created an actor reference to track the enemy each one clones, we'll want to  
create an actor [set] to track the multiple enemies cloned.  
Open one of the Enemy Manager's scripts, and, down where we previously made the actor *reference,  
make a new actor [set] - name it enemies cloned.  
Where we were previously setting the *enemy clone reference, delete that line and add a new set [set]  
command, adjusting it to include ^cloned.  
Above, where we control the old actor reference, add a new check value line, and check if the [enemies  
cloned] set has less than 3 members.  
Move the logic from the else section of the control into the check value, so we change behaviors and  
clone a new enemy whenever there are less than 3 members.  
8 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
We're almost there! This would work for a bit - 3 enemies, indeed, would be created, but the problem is that we  
add to a set but never exclude from the set. Even though the enemy actors shut down when they're killed, that  
won't stop other scripts from hanging on to them. Let's use for each as a method to check if each member of  
our actor [set] is still alive.  
Add a new for each command, and set the parameter to be for each in [enemies cloned].  
Drag the control line into the for each and set it to try to control ^current in [for each].  
In the control's else, add a new set [set] command, and set [enemies cloned] to exclude ^current in  
[for each]'.  
9 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
Remove the old, unneeded *enemy clone actor reference, and the ﬁnished script should look as follows.  
Build and launch, and you should now see a huge pileup of enemies as new ones spawn every second until  
there are 3 per spawner. If you kill one, another one should spawn a second after the old one shuts down.  
Perfect!  
Level Variables  
So far, we've been using Enemy Actor's [clones] as a way of looking for enemies, but it's a fairly inﬂexible  
way to do things. What if we wanted to add a diﬀerent enemy type? You may also notice that the player will still  
autoaim and do collision checks with enemies even as they're dying. We could solve that by making sure the  
projectile doesn't remove itself if the enemies it's hitting are already dead, but it would be nice if we had more  
control over what the player should really be shooting at. Right now, we're really tied to how that [clones] set  
works, and it's limited to clones of that one actor, Enemy Actor.  
We've learned about creating an actor [set] with the spawners. We could look for enemies to hit by looking at  
each spawner's own actor set, but that's still awfully speciﬁc and unwieldy. What we really need is a level  
variable (also sometimes called global variable) actor set, which exists in the level's space as opposed to  
within a script's. When you create a level variable, all scripts in the level can access it.  
In the actor panel, we've used the New and Dup buttons (and you can probably infer what the Del button does -  
it deletes), but there are a few more buttons, below.  
10 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
The *Ref button creates a *reference.  
The Folder Icon button creates a [set].  
The asterisked Folder Icon creates a [set] *reference.  
Though we've just used the actor panel so far, all the other panels have similar buttons - you can create a  
*reference in a level to a sound, for example.  
Creating an Enemies Set  
Click the Folder Icon button to create a new actor set in the level. A New Set entry will appear with a  
folder icon denoting it.  
Type in a name for the set - let's call it Enemy Actors - and hit enter. The [set] will sort itself in the list,  
below the placed actors.  
Sets created in this way can actually by populated by dragging and dropping actors into them. This is the  
equivalent of saying, at the startup of the level, set [set] <your set> to include <the actor>. We often use  
these sets for organization, especially when levels have hundreds of objects in them. You can even put sets  
within sets, but remember: sets cannot contain other sets, just objects. In the case of nesting, it is the same  
as saying: make each of these sets include these members, on startup.  
Utilizing the Enemies Set  
Now that we have a level [set] for our enemies, we can start using it. While the enemy spawner keeps a local  
set for its own purpose and has commands in there to maintain it, the easiest way to make sure any given  
enemy gets put into the set will be to add a set [set] command in the startup of the enemy script. If we make  
more enemies down the road, it's just a matter of making sure to replicate that line in the startup.  
Open the Enemy Actor's script and add a new set [set] command to the STARTUP.  
Adjust the line to set [Enemy Actors] to include myself. Since the [Enemy Actor] set is in the level, it  
11 of 13  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 4.4: Intermediate Scripting 4 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.4:_Intermediate...  
will be under the (ACTORS) submenu.  
The ﬁnal line should look as follows.  
Remember the rule of sets: if you put something in the set, you're obligated to exclude it. It won't do it itself!  
We'll do this by adding an exclude line to the shutdown.  
Copy-and-paste the set [set] line (or create a new one) in the SHUTDOWN and set it to exclude myself.  
Perfect! Now there's a level [set] that will be populated as enemies startup, and emptied as they shut down.  
Now, we need to put it to use.  
Open the Projectile's script and adjust the ﬁnd subset line where we check for Enemy Actor's [clones]  
to check for the new [Enemy Actors] set instead.  
Open the Player's script and adjust the check fov line to check within the [Enemy Actors] set.  
Now we're getting really general with our powerful scripts. We have a player who can aim at a set of arbitrary  
targets, a projectile that can impact arbitrary targets (and attempt to hurt them), and enemies that ﬂag  
themselves as being targetable! If you wanted, at this point, you could even create a whole other enemy with a  
diﬀerent behavior. So long as it has a hurt value and put itself in the [EnemyTargets] set, the player would be  
able to interact with it in the exact same way.  
At this point, there are two directions you can go: further into levels or further into script. You have pretty  
much all the scripting chops you'll need to accomplish most of the work you'll want to do to make levels.  
If you want to broaden your knowledge, learn more about the diﬀerent types of objects and assets in our  
system like sounds and particles, along with how designers really build levels and some of the features that  
help with that, the next few chapters will cover that. You may proceed to the next chapter.  
However, if you want to learn about some of the even more advanced features like macros and structures, you  
could, instead, jump ahead before you cover the aforementioned level features. None of the other pages in this  
guide will assume you've read this - it's only there if you want it - and you can always come back. You may jump  
ahead to Advanced Scripting.
